etymology.description: phonetic 𦎫 (shú) + semantic 丮 (to grasp) -> modern script corrupted to 享 (xiǎng, "enjoy") + 丸 (wán, "pill") -> resulting form 孰 (shú, "cooked; ripe")
etymology.interpretation: The character 孰 (shú) originated from a combination of the components 𦎫 (phonetic value) and 丮 (semantic meaning of grasping or holding). Over time, the script evolved, with 𦎫 becoming 享 (used for its phonetic value) and 丮 transforming into 丸, leading to the current form that retains the sense of something that is fully processed or ripe as if grasped or completed.

examples.chinese: 这个苹果已经孰(這個蘋果已經孰)。
当果子孰时(當果子孰時)，它会掉落(它會掉落)。
examples.pinyin: Dāng guǒzi shú shí, tā huì diàoluò.
examples.english: When the fruit is ripe, it will fall.

examples.chinese: 他在等肉孰(他在等肉孰)，才能开始吃(才能開始吃)。
examples.pinyin: Tā zài děng ròu shú, cái néng kāishǐ chī.
examples.english: He is waiting for the meat to be cooked before he starts eating.
